text: The current generation cannot measure success in the big house/big car, especially the former, because the mechanics of capitalism has permitted the haves (rich) to a) hoard massive amounts of wealth b) drive the prices of property hugely higher and c) control the rate of increase of earnings such that it is vastly less than the increases in costs.  Together these have made such measures of success virtually impossible for the current generation to realise.

author: @JohnBoen
text:
I went through the maybe I am not right phase about 8 years ago.

The solution was to ignore all information that did not go through peer review.

I also give speculative arguments exactly the time they deserve. 

Seems really cut and dried to me.

author: @chrisoregan2932
text: Rob you know chemistry... i as a layman, i do not understand why CO2 is bad, it is heavier than oxygen so will sink to the floor. yet it is in the atmosphere warming the planet? i don't get it

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text:
If all the CO2 could sink, there would be no issue as it would return to earth and get recycled by plants, but even though it is heavier, being a gas it easily gets mixed with air by the slightest breeze and also tends to rise at it is typically released at a much higher temperature than ambient air.
The general consensus is that CO2 becomes a problem when it accumulates in the upper atmosphere, as it traps heat which should have been radiated back into space, altering air currents in the process. The chain of events leading to climate change is actually quite convoluted, but the ongoing increase in CO2 levels can be correlated with human activities, mostly industrial-scale activities, which is something we *can* address (and should have addressed from day 1) but still haven't addressed yet. Instead, people get carbon taxes and industries get carbon credits. Sounds familiar? I could swear I saw that flawed model elsewhere!

author: @superktmduke
text: We're in this mess because our leaders are overthinking.

replies.author: @JohnBoen
replies.text:
23% deny climate change. Half of our (US) government denies the science.  They are overrepresented by a factor of 2. It isn't America - - it is one party. The GOP denies climate science.

37% of Christians in America do not believe the science.

60% of Americans claim to be Christians.

Christians who deny Climate change make up 22% of the population.

22 out of 23 of the climate science deniers in the USA claim to be Christians.

This tells me the problem is almost entirely limited to Christians and Republicans.

author: @ffaubert1
text:
Great video as always Robert. 

That was kind of the take away I got from yesterday's video as well. I read the some of the comments and thought some may have missed the message.

You are also correct that if two camps are arguing polar opposite points of view that are both supported by real facts, there is a flaw in the logic on one side or the other. Quite possibly both.

Thanks again Rob for all of the time and effort that you put into all of your videos. It does not go unnoticed.

author: @lachlanraidal5100
text: I think a huge part of the climate change problem is the fact that fossil fuel companies can afford to influence elections and policy. The public consensus is overwhelmingly in favour of taking major actions to avert climate change, so that supports the idea that humanity is at the heart of things good. However we have problem with misaligned systems of power that don't always follow the consensus, along with the abuse of copious amounts of wealth by parties heavily invested in our non-renewable energy sector. Whilst I love technology and believe it can greatly help us create change, I have also come to reckon with the fact that a great deal of grassroots social and political change will be needed from us to democratise our grids if we want to see these solutions actually implemented at scale.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Typically what happens is that the rich entitle themselves to take from the poor because they think themselves more important than the poor by virtue of their wealth.

I suspect that eventually, the powers that be will do something in the name of "saving the planet" that is so stupid, it threatens to literally destroy the earth, which is why we read such a statement at the end of Revelation 11:18.

Quite often, people are not as smart as they think they are.

Revelation 11:18: And the nations were angry, and thy wrath is come, and the time of the dead, that they should be judged, and that thou shouldest give reward unto thy servants the prophets, and to the saints, and them that fear thy name, small and great; and >>>shouldest destroy them which destroy the earth<<<.

author: @kensmith5694
text: In many ways, the US's national highway system is more important than putting a man on the moon.  The same can be said for the rural electrification project.  A lot of today's prosperity in the US is the result of those two efforts.

replies.author: @bas_6265
replies.text: While the highway system and rural electrification project were crucial for the country's prosperity, space exploration has also sparked many groundbreaking ideas and opportunities. Innovations in satellites, medicine, materials, electronics, water management, and remote surgery a.o. have been game-changers. It's not about one being more important than the other; they all work together, each contributing to progress.

text: Well, there are probably good ways to take CO_2 out of the air that don't require nuclear explosions.  (Can't remember if you've done videos on those, but there ideas out there, of course, including on Youtube.)  I suspect that's going to be the way forward.  People don't really want to use less energy, except where something can be made more efficient pain-free.

author: @zmavrick
text: Excellent insight. Mankind is not all seeing or all knowing and often the things we do have some negative consequences even when they are done with the best of intentions.

author: @AORD72
text: Abundant consumption is not a problem if you have machines that can recycle everything. Right now our recycling isn't great, but the future might have 100% recycling of all waste into new products.

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: One also has to consider the energy used in over-producing things that we really don't need or use. Even though the materials used in the production of something may be near 100% recyclable the energy used to make it can't be recovered and is wasted. It is important and in our interest to minimize energy consumption that is wasteful.

replies.author: @AORD72
replies.text:
@William_Hada Good point. How ever I also imagine a future of abundant energy. In 200 years technology has moved at a fanatic pace. And it seems to be accelerating, especially now humans have access to so much knowledge via the internet. Then there is the AI systems that could become AGI. Imagine machines with the intelligence of 1000 humans or more.

There is enough solar radiation to power humanity many times over. Fusion reactors are a real possibility.
